I used to buy groceries impulsively and let half of them rot in my fridge, which honestly makes me cringe now. These days I plan my meals for the week and only buy what I'll actually eat. I've also started turning off lights and unplugging chargers out of habit. None of it is dramatic, but becoming aware of my own waste was really the turning point for me.

Honestly, I think it's a shared responsibility, but the biggest weight has to fall on companies and governments. Individuals like me can recycle and save energy, and we absolutely should, but our impact is small compared to giant corporations and their emissions. Governments have the power to set rules that actually force change at a large scale. That said, I don't think individuals are off the hook, our choices add up and push companies to do better. So everyone plays a part, but the powerful players really need to lead.

I'm cautiously optimistic, honestly. On one hand, the news about climate change can feel genuinely scary and overwhelming. But on the other hand, I see my whole generation caring about this in a way our grandparents never did. Renewable energy is getting cheaper every year, and people are demanding change loudly. I remember my campus switching entirely to solar power last year, and small wins like that give me real hope. Progress feels frustratingly slow, but it is happening. I choose to believe we can still turn things around.

questions: What kind of work do you imagine yourself doing, and what appeals to you about it?
What matters more to you in a job, a good salary or work you enjoy? Why?
Tell me about someone whose career you admire.
Do you think people should change careers if they're unhappy, even later in life? Explain.
answers: I imagine myself working in data analysis, ideally somewhere I can turn messy numbers into insights that help people make better decisions. What appeals to me most is the mix of logic and creativity, it's like solving a puzzle, except the answer actually matters in the real world. I love that quiet satisfaction when a confusing dataset suddenly reveals a clear story. I also want work that keeps challenging me, because I get bored pretty easily with routine. A career where I never stop learning honestly sounds perfect to me.

For me, enjoying the work matters more than the salary, as long as the pay covers my basic needs. I've realized we spend the majority of our waking lives working, so dreading every single Monday would be a miserable way to live. I saw this with my aunt, who left a high-paying but soul-crushing job to become a teacher for a lot less money, and she's honestly never been happier. That really shaped my thinking. Money matters, of course, but no amount of it makes up for hating what you do daily.

Someone whose career I really admire is my former professor. She started out as an ordinary engineer, but she was genuinely curious and kept teaching herself new fields, eventually becoming a respected researcher and mentor. What I admire isn't just her success, it's that she stayed humble and always made time to help struggling students like me. She once spent an entire afternoon patiently explaining a concept I just couldn't grasp. To me, that combination of real achievement and genuine generosity is exactly the kind of career I hope to build.

Yes, I strongly believe people should change careers if they're truly unhappy, even later in life. Life is simply too short to spend decades miserable for the sake of stability. I know it's scary, there's real risk, lost income, and the fear of starting over, but staying stuck has its own quieter cost on your health and spirit. My neighbor switched from banking to running a small bakery at fifty, and everyone thought he was crazy. Now he's the happiest I've ever seen him. It's never too late to choose fulfillment.

questions: Is there a tradition your family keeps that you look forward to?
How did that tradition get started, as far as you know?
Which family traditions would you want to pass on someday?
Do you think traditions matter as much now as they used to? Why or why not?
answers: The tradition I look forward to most is our big family reunion dinner every Lunar New Year. No matter how busy everyone is or how far away they've moved, all my relatives gather at my grandparents' house for one enormous meal. I love it because it's genuinely the only time of year I see all my cousins in one room. We stay up late playing cards, eating way too much, and catching up on everyone's lives. That warm, crowded, noisy house is honestly my favorite feeling in the entire world.

As far as I know, this tradition started with my great-grandmother, decades ago. The story goes that after some hard years when the family was scattered and struggling, she insisted that everyone come home at least once a year, no excuses allowed. She wanted to make absolutely sure the family never drifted completely apart. That first reunion was apparently just a simple meal, but it stuck, and it's been passed down ever since. I love knowing that something I enjoy so much today grew out of one woman's determination to keep us together.

The one I'd absolutely want to pass on is that same New Year reunion dinner. In a world where everyone's so busy and scattered, I think having one guaranteed time each year to reconnect is honestly priceless. I'd want my future kids to grow up knowing their cousins and grandparents the same way I did. I might update a few things, maybe add new dishes or games, but the heart of it, everyone gathering under one roof, is something I'd protect and carry forward no matter what.

I think traditions still matter, maybe even more than before, precisely because life has gotten so fast and scattered. These days people move away, work constantly, and communicate mostly through screens, so it's incredibly easy to drift apart. Traditions force us to pause and physically come together, which we rarely do otherwise. I'll admit some old customs feel outdated and naturally fade, and that's completely fine. But the ones that bring people closer are genuinely valuable. In our disconnected modern world, I'd argue we need those anchoring moments more than ever.

questions: How do you usually feel when someone criticizes your work?
Can you describe a time when feedback actually helped you improve?
Do you think it's better to get honest criticism or gentle encouragement?
How do you decide whether a piece of criticism is worth acting on?
answers: Honestly, my first reaction is usually a little defensive. Like, when my professor covered my essay in red ink last term, my stomach kind of dropped and I wanted to argue. But after a few minutes I calm down and start reading it properly. I've learned that the sting fades fast, and what's left is usually pretty useful. So now I try to just say thanks in the moment and process my feelings later, because reacting emotionally in front of people never really helps me or makes me look good.

Sure. Last year a classmate told me my presentations were hard to follow because I crammed way too much text onto every slide. At first I felt embarrassed, but she was totally right. So I redesigned my next talk, one idea per slide, big visuals, almost no words. And the difference was huge, people actually asked questions afterward and said they got my point. That one comment basically changed how I present everything now. If she'd just been polite and said nothing, I'd still be boring rooms to death.

For me, honest criticism wins, as long as it's kind. Encouragement feels nice in the moment, but it doesn't tell me what to fix, so I don't grow from it. When someone's straight with me, even if it stings a bit, I actually know where I stand. That said, tone really matters. There's a difference between honest and harsh. A good critic points at the work, not the person. So I'd say give me the truth, just wrap it in a little respect, and I'll take it every time.

I ask myself a couple of quick questions. First, does this person actually know the area, or are they just reacting? My coding mentor's feedback carries way more weight than a random comment online. Second, do I keep hearing the same thing from different people? If three folks all say my writing is too wordy, that's clearly a real pattern, not just one opinion. If it's a one-off from someone with no context, I'll note it but not lose sleep. Basically I look for expertise and repetition before I change anything.

questions: What's a skill you learned on your own, without a teacher?
How did you go about teaching yourself that skill?
What was the hardest part of learning without any guidance?
Would you recommend self-teaching to other people? Why or why not?
answers: I taught myself to cook, actually. Growing up, my parents handled all the meals, so when I moved into my own apartment I basically couldn't make anything beyond instant noodles. It got embarrassing fast. So I decided to just figure it out. Now I can put together a decent stir-fry, some soups, even a couple of desserts. It started as pure survival, honestly, but it turned into something I genuinely enjoy. There's something really satisfying about eating a meal you made yourself instead of ordering takeout for the tenth night in a row.

Mostly YouTube and a lot of trial and error, to be honest. I'd pick one dish, watch two or three videos of different people making it, and then just try it in my kitchen. My first few attempts were pretty rough, I burned rice more than once and once set off the smoke alarm. But I kept a little note on my phone of what went wrong each time, like too much heat or not enough salt. Cooking the same dish repeatedly, tweaking one thing each round, is really what got me there.

The hardest part was not knowing why something failed. When you have a teacher, they can look at your dish and instantly say, oh, your pan wasn't hot enough. But alone, I'd just get a bad result and have no clue which of ten things caused it. So I wasted a lot of ingredients guessing. I had to become my own detective, changing one variable at a time. It was slow and honestly a bit frustrating, but weirdly, that struggle made the lessons stick way harder than being spoon-fed the answer would have.

Yeah, definitely, with one caveat. Self-teaching builds this confidence that you can tackle almost anything if you're patient, and that mindset spills into everything else. But I'd tell people to pick skills where mistakes are cheap and safe, like cooking or coding, not something where a wrong move is dangerous. And I'd say don't be too proud to check in with an expert occasionally. I probably could've saved myself weeks if I'd just asked one experienced friend a few questions early on. So teach yourself, but stay humble about it.

questions: Is there a particular place where you go to relax?
What is it about that place that helps you unwind?
How often do you get to spend time there?
Do you think everyone needs a place like that? Why?
answers: For me it's this small lake near my university. There's a wooden bench on the quiet side where hardly anyone goes, and I've kind of claimed it as mine. Whenever I'm stressed about exams or just feeling overwhelmed, I grab a coffee and walk over there. It's maybe fifteen minutes from my dorm. Something about sitting by the water, watching the ducks and the light on the surface, just resets my brain. I've made some of my biggest decisions on that bench, honestly. It's become my little escape from campus chaos.

It's mainly the quiet and the water, I think. My daily life is so noisy, notifications buzzing, people talking, deadlines everywhere, so a place with no screens and no demands feels like a huge relief. The lake moves slowly, and that rhythm kind of slows me down too. My shoulders literally drop when I sit there. There's also no expectation to do anything, which is rare. I'm not being productive or social, I'm just existing for a bit. That permission to do nothing is honestly what makes it so restful for me.

Not as often as I'd like, sadly. During busy weeks I might only make it once, usually on a Sunday morning when campus is dead quiet. In exam season it can slip to zero, which is ironic because that's exactly when I need it most. But I've started treating it almost like an appointment, blocking out an hour so I actually go. When I keep that habit, I notice I'm way calmer and more focused all week. When I skip it, everything feels heavier. So I'm trying to protect that time.

Yeah, I really do think everyone needs one. Life throws so much stress at us, and if you never have a space to decompress, it just piles up until you burn out. Your place doesn't have to be a lake, it could be a gym, a library corner, even a car. The point is having somewhere your brain associates with calm, so just going there flips a switch. I've watched friends who have no such place get completely fried during finals. A little sanctuary isn't a luxury, honestly, it's basic maintenance for your mind.

questions: How important is it to you to keep your promises?
Can you tell me about a time you kept a promise even when it was hard?
What do you do if you realize you can't keep a promise you made?
Do you think people take promises less seriously these days?
answers: It's really important to me, maybe more than most things. I think your word is basically your reputation, so if people can't trust what you say, nothing else really matters. Growing up, my grandfather always said a person is only as good as their promises, and that stuck with me. So I try to be careful about what I commit to in the first place. I'd rather say no upfront than say yes and let someone down later. Once I do promise something, I treat it as non-negotiable, even when it becomes inconvenient.

Sure. I once promised a friend I'd help him move apartments on a Saturday. Then that same week I got invited to a concert I'd wanted to see for ages, tickets and everything, on the exact same day. I was so tempted to bail. But I'd given my word, and he was counting on me because he had almost nobody else. So I skipped the concert and spent the day carrying boxes up four flights of stairs. He was genuinely grateful, and honestly, that trust between us is worth way more than one night out.

First thing, I tell the person as early as possible. Nothing's worse than leaving someone hanging until the last minute when they were relying on you. So I'm honest, I explain what changed, and I apologize properly without making excuses. Then I try to fix it, either by finding an alternative or helping them find another solution. Like if I can't make a shift, I'll try to line up someone to cover for me first. Breaking a promise is bad, but breaking it silently and leaving someone stranded is way worse in my book.

I think it's a mixed picture, honestly. In some ways, yeah, casual promises get thrown around and forgotten, like people say let's hang out and never follow up. Texting makes it really easy to just cancel with one tap. But I don't think people care less about the promises that actually matter. When it's something serious, most folks I know still show up. Maybe the problem is we make too many small commitments we never intended to keep. If we promised less but meant it more, I think the whole thing would feel more solid.

questions: Do you consider yourself a creative person?
Can you describe a time you solved a problem in an unusual way?
Where do you think good ideas usually come from?
Do you think creativity can be learned, or are people just born with it?
answers: Hmm, I'd say I'm creative in a practical way, not an artistic one. I can't paint or write poetry to save my life, but I'm pretty good at finding workarounds when something's broken or stuck. Like, I'm the person friends call when their plan falls apart and they need a quick fix. I think creativity isn't only about art, it's about connecting ideas that don't obviously go together. In that sense, yeah, I'm creative. I just express it through solving everyday problems rather than making something beautiful to hang on a wall.

Sure. In a group project, our shared document kept getting messed up because five of us were editing at once and overwriting each other. People were frustrated. Instead of arguing about rules, I suggested we assign each person a color and a specific section, and nobody touches anyone else's color. It sounds simple, but it completely killed the chaos overnight. Everyone knew exactly what was theirs. It wasn't some genius invention, just reframing a people problem as a design problem. Sometimes the unusual move is just stepping back and changing the setup instead of pushing harder.

Honestly, I think good ideas usually come from boredom and from mixing different worlds. My best ideas never show up when I'm grinding at my desk, they pop up in the shower or on a walk, when my mind's wandering. That downtime lets thoughts collide randomly. The other big source is combining fields, like when someone applies a trick from cooking to organizing their studies. Fresh ideas are often just old ideas from one area dropped into a new one. So I try to stay curious about stuff outside my lane, because that's where the sparks come from.

I really believe it can be learned, mostly. Sure, some people seem naturally more imaginative, but creativity is basically a muscle you build by exposing yourself to lots of different things and then practicing making connections. The more raw material you feed your brain, books, hobbies, random conversations, the more it has to remix. I've watched myself get more creative just by asking what if more often and not fearing dumb ideas. The people who seem born creative usually just gave themselves permission to play. So no, I don't think it's some rare gift only a few have.

topic: A tool or app you rely on
questions: Is there an app or tool you use every single day?
How did that tool change the way you do things?
What would happen if you suddenly couldn't use it anymore?
Do you ever worry about depending too much on these tools?
answers: Yeah, for me it's my calendar app, hands down. I open it probably twenty times a day. Every class, deadline, meeting, even little things like calling my mom, all of it goes in there the second it comes up. Before I started using it seriously, I was constantly forgetting stuff and double-booking myself. Now my whole life basically lives on that screen. It's not fancy or exciting, but it's the one tool I genuinely couldn't function without. If my phone died with no backup, I'd honestly feel a bit lost for the day.

It completely changed how I handle my mind, honestly. I used to try to remember everything in my head, and I was always low-key anxious that I was forgetting something important. Once I started dumping every commitment into the calendar, my brain finally relaxed, because I trusted the system to remember for me. That freed up a ton of mental energy for actually doing the work instead of tracking it. I also stopped over-committing, because seeing my week laid out visually shows me instantly when I'm about to overload myself. It made me way more reliable, too.

I'd struggle at first, no question. The first few days I'd probably miss a couple of things and feel scattered, because I've outsourced so much of my memory to it. But I think I'd adapt. I'd go back to a paper planner, which I actually used in high school and kind of miss. The core habit, writing things down instead of trusting my memory, would survive even without the app. So losing the tool would hurt short-term, but the underlying system is mine. The app is just the most convenient container for it.

A little, yeah, it does cross my mind. There's this worry that if I lean on tools for everything, my own memory and mental sharpness get lazy. Like, I can barely remember phone numbers anymore because my phone does it for me. But I've kind of made peace with it. I figure it's fine to offload boring stuff like dates and numbers so my brain has room for thinking, creating, actually solving problems. The key, I think, is not outsourcing the important skills, only the tedious ones. As long as I keep that line, I'm okay with it.

questions: Do you usually feel nervous when you have to speak in front of a group?
How do you prepare before giving a presentation or a speech?
Can you describe a time when you had to speak in public and it went well?
Do you think being nervous before speaking is a bad thing, or can it help you?
answers: Honestly, yeah, I do get pretty nervous. The moment a teacher says we're presenting, my heart starts racing and my hands get a little cold. I think it's because I worry about forgetting my words or people judging me. But I've noticed that once I actually start talking, like after the first thirty seconds, the nerves kind of fade away and I settle in. So it's really the anticipation that gets me, not the speaking itself. It's gotten a bit better with practice, but I'd be lying if I said it ever fully goes away.

Well, the first thing I always do is write out my main points on a small card, not the whole script, just the key ideas so I don't ramble. Then I practice out loud in my room, sometimes in front of the mirror, which feels a little silly but really helps. I also time myself so I know I won't go over. On the actual day, I get there early, drink some water, and take a few slow deep breaths. Having that routine makes me feel like I'm in control, and that calms me down a lot.

Sure. Last semester I had to present a research project on renewable energy to my whole class. I was terrified beforehand, but I'd practiced a ton, so once I got going the words just came. What really helped was that I made eye contact with a couple of friendly faces in the room instead of staring at my slides. At the end, a few classmates actually asked me questions because they were curious, and my professor said my explanation was really clear. Walking back to my seat, I felt this huge wave of relief and honestly a little proud.

You know, I actually think a little nervousness can be a good thing. When I'm nervous, it means I care about doing well, and that pushes me to prepare more thoroughly instead of just winging it. The adrenaline can even make me more alert and energetic when I'm up there. The problem is only when it gets so intense that I freeze or forget everything. So for me, the goal isn't to kill the nerves completely, it's to channel them. A calm, prepared kind of nervous energy usually makes my delivery sharper, not worse.

topic: A person who shaped your values
questions: Is there someone in your life who has really influenced the way you think?
What is one value or lesson that person taught you?
Can you give an example of a time they showed you that value in action?
How has knowing this person changed the way you live day to day?
answers: Definitely my grandmother. I grew up spending a lot of time at her house, and she had this quiet, steady way of looking at the world that really rubbed off on me. She never lectured anybody, she just lived a certain way and you couldn't help but notice. She's the kind of person who treats the cashier and the boss with exactly the same respect. I think a lot of who I am, especially how I handle stress and how I treat people, comes straight from watching her. She shaped me more than any teacher ever did.

The biggest thing she taught me is patience, and along with it, kindness that doesn't expect anything back. She used to say that you never really know what someone else is carrying, so it costs you nothing to be gentle. That stuck with me. She also taught me to finish what I start, even when it's boring or hard. She'd say a job half done is worse than not starting. Those two things, patience with people and stubbornness with tasks, are probably the values I lean on the most now, honestly.

There's one moment I'll never forget. A neighbor of hers was going through a really rough time, kind of bitter and short-tempered with everyone. Most people just avoided him. But my grandmother kept bringing him food every few days, no big speeches, just quietly leaving it at his door. After a couple of months, he completely softened and became one of her closest friends. Watching that taught me more about patience and kindness than any words could. She didn't do it to look good, she just genuinely believed people deserve another chance.

It's changed a lot of small everyday things, actually. When I'm frustrated with a friend or a classmate, I try to pause and imagine what they might be dealing with, just like she would. I'm way more likely to give someone the benefit of the doubt now. And when I'm tempted to quit a project halfway, I hear her voice about finishing what I start, and I push through. It's funny, she's not physically with me most days, but her way of thinking is kind of always running in the background of my decisions.

questions: Have you ever had to work closely with someone you found difficult?
What made working with that person challenging for you?
How did you handle the situation and keep things moving forward?
Looking back, what did that experience teach you about dealing with people?
answers: Oh, definitely. In a group project last year, I was paired with a classmate who was really hard to work with. He had strong opinions about everything and would kind of shut down other people's ideas before we'd even finished explaining them. At the same time, he wasn't very reliable about actually doing his part on time. So it was this frustrating mix of being very controlling but not very dependable. I remember dreading our meetings at first. But we couldn't switch groups, so I had to figure out how to make it work.

The hardest part was honestly the communication. Every time I suggested something, he'd interrupt or say why it wouldn't work, so I started feeling like there was no point in speaking up. It got a little discouraging. On top of that, when he missed deadlines, the rest of us had to scramble to cover his sections, which felt unfair. What made it really challenging was that he wasn't a bad person, he genuinely cared about the grade, he just had this abrasive style that clashed with how the rest of us liked to collaborate. Finding common ground took real effort.

What ended up working was talking to him one on one, away from the group. Instead of accusing him, I asked what part of the project he was actually most excited about, and it turned out he loved the data analysis. So we gave him that piece, where his strong opinions were actually useful, and it played to his strengths. I also started sending gentle reminders a day before each deadline, which cut down the missed work a lot. Once he felt heard and had a clear role, he became way easier and honestly contributed some of our best material.

It taught me that difficult usually isn't the whole story. A lot of the friction came from mismatched styles, not from him being a bad teammate. Once I stopped taking his bluntness personally and looked for what he was actually good at, things shifted completely. I also learned that a private, calm conversation solves way more than complaining behind someone's back. Now, whenever I hit friction with somebody, my first instinct is to get curious about why they act that way instead of just labeling them as annoying. That mindset has genuinely made teamwork a lot smoother for me.

questions: Are you someone who's curious about science or how the universe works?
Is there a particular scientific topic or discovery that fascinates you?
Where do you usually go to learn about science that interests you?
Do you think ordinary people should care about things like space exploration?
answers: Yeah, I'd say I'm pretty curious, especially about the big questions. I'm not a science genius or anything, but I love that feeling of learning something that makes the world feel larger. Space in particular grabs me. Every time I look up at a clear night sky, I get this mix of feeling tiny and feeling amazed at the same time. I think curiosity is kind of my default setting. If I don't understand how something works, whether it's the weather or black holes, it kind of nags at me until I go read about it.

For sure, black holes fascinate me the most. The whole idea that there's something so dense that not even light can escape it just breaks my brain in the best way. I remember first learning that time actually slows down near one, and I couldn't stop thinking about it for days. It sounds like science fiction, but it's real and there's actual math behind it. What I love is that even the smartest physicists still don't fully understand what happens inside. There's this huge mystery sitting right there in the universe, and that gap is what makes it so thrilling.

Mostly online, honestly. I watch a lot of science channels on YouTube where they explain complicated ideas in a really visual, simple way, which is perfect for someone like me without a physics degree. I also follow a couple of space agencies on social media, so when there's a new discovery or a rocket launch, it pops up in my feed. Occasionally I'll read a popular science book if a topic really hooks me. And when there's a documentary about the cosmos, I'm all in. I like sources that keep the wonder but don't drown me in equations.

Absolutely, I think they should. I know some people say we've got enough problems here on Earth to spend money on space, and I get that. But so much of the technology we use every day, like GPS, weather forecasting, even certain medical tools, came out of space research. Beyond the practical stuff, though, I think exploration feeds something deeper in us. It reminds us to keep asking big questions and pushing our limits as a species. A world that stops being curious about the universe feels smaller and sadder to me. So yeah, it's worth caring about.

questions: Which season of the year do you like the most?
How does the weather usually affect your mood or your energy?
Is there a type of weather you really don't enjoy? Why?
Do you think the weather where you live shapes how people there behave?
answers: Autumn is easily my favorite. There's just something about that crisp, cool air that makes me feel alive and focused. The summer heat kind of drains me, and by the time fall rolls around, I feel like I can finally breathe again. I love the colors too, all the leaves turning orange and red, and that cozy feeling of putting on a light jacket. It's also when the school year gets into a good rhythm, so it feels productive. Honestly, if I could live in a permanent October, I think I'd be a very happy person.

The weather affects me way more than I'd like to admit. On a bright, sunny day, I wake up with tons of energy and I actually want to get things done and go outside. But when it's gray and rainy for several days in a row, I notice I get kind of sluggish and unmotivated, like I just want to stay in bed. I've learned to work with it, though. On gloomy days I'll turn on all the lights, play upbeat music, and it lifts me a bit. Still, sunshine is basically free motivation for me.

Yeah, I really can't stand heavy humidity, that thick, sticky heat where the air feels heavy. When it's like that, I feel sweaty and irritable no matter what I do, and it's impossible to get comfortable. Even sitting still, you feel exhausted. Cold I can handle because I can always add another layer, but with humidity there's no escaping it unless you're in air conditioning. It also makes it hard to sleep, which then wrecks my whole next day. So if I had to pick my least favorite, it's a hot, humid, breezeless afternoon for sure.

I really do think it shapes people. Where I'm from, the winters are long and cold, and I've noticed people tend to be more indoorsy and maybe a little more reserved during those months. Then the second the weather warms up, everyone's suddenly out in the parks, chatting, way more social and open. It's like the whole city's personality changes with the temperature. I've read that places with lots of sunshine often have this more relaxed, outdoor lifestyle, and that matches what I've seen. So yeah, I think climate quietly influences the rhythm of how a whole community lives.

topic: Keeping in touch with old friends
questions: Do you still stay in contact with friends from your childhood or earlier schools?
How do you usually keep those friendships alive when you're busy or far apart?
Can you tell me about an old friend you've managed to stay close with?
Do you think it's worth the effort to hold on to old friendships as life changes?
answers: I do with a few of them, though I'll admit it takes effort. After we all went off to different universities, it got harder, because everyone's schedule filled up and we're spread across different cities now. There are some friends I've sadly drifted from, and we only like each other's posts now. But there's a small core group I've really held onto, and those friendships mean the world to me. I think as I've gotten older, I've realized quality matters way more than quantity, so I'd rather deeply keep three old friends than loosely know twenty.

Mostly through little consistent things rather than grand gestures. We have a group chat that's been alive for years where we just share memes, random thoughts, and the occasional big news. That keeps a background connection going even during busy weeks. Then every couple of months, we'll try to do a video call, and honestly it feels like no time has passed. When we're actually in the same city, we make a real point of meeting up, even if it's just a quick coffee. I've learned that friendships don't need constant contact, they just need you to not disappear completely.

Sure, my best friend from middle school, we've known each other for over ten years now. We went to totally different colleges and even different fields, she's in medicine and I'm not, but somehow we just clicked back then and never lost it. What's cool is that whenever we meet up, we pick right back up like we saw each other yesterday. She was there for me during some really hard times as a teenager, and I was there for her, and that shared history creates this bond you just can't build overnight with new people. She's basically family at this point.

Honestly, yes, completely worth it. Old friends knew you before you became whoever you are now, so they see the real you without all the polish. There's a comfort in that you can't fake with newer people. They also keep you grounded, they'll happily remind you of embarrassing old stories so you don't take yourself too seriously. Sure, it takes effort, and some friendships naturally fade, and that's okay. But the ones worth keeping give back so much more than they cost. As life keeps changing around me, having those steady, long-term people is something I really treasure.
